metadata.name: 🏆 Total Drama Franchise Rankings - Complete Cast
improvements: Titles + emojis refreshed to match character archetypes
Kept full reasoning blocks + stats fields for every player

rankings.name: Alejandro
rankings.title: The Snake Charmer
rankings.reasoning: 3 FINALS APPEARANCES (franchise rarity). WINNER of S4 (7-6-1). MOST challenge wins in the franchise (13). Constant heat all game (25 votes against). Top jury pull in the database (12 finalist votes). Strategic mastermind (ranked #1). Saved by Ezekiel's idol play (Ep 8). (S2).

rankings.title: The Ice-Cold Closer
rankings.reasoning: Jacques owns one of the cleanest résumés in the franchise: a win, two more Final 4 finishes, 11 challenge wins, and two idols across three seasons. His S6 run was a clinic in composure, using late immunities and calm threat management to secure a dominant 7-1-0 crown. The only thing keeping him out of the very top tier of legacies is volatility — his S7 Final 4 collapse and quit turned a near-lock endgame into a cautionary tale, and S12 showed he could still be hit with votes when the room hardened against him.
rankings.name: Jacques
Bowie
rankings.title: The Velvet Guillotine
rankings.reasoning: Bowie has become the franchise's cleanest modern strategist: a winner, a runner-up, and the most advantage-literate player on the board. His S9 win was a masterpiece of hoarded power and surgical betrayal, and S10 nearly repeated the trick as he formed The Movement, cut Mickey early, steered Stephanie's ambassador boot, buried Scott at merge, and still reached the finale with five advantages played. The only flaw is visibility: by the end, too many jurors could point to him as the architect, and Jasmine turned his chess match into a footrace he could not control.

rankings.name: James
rankings.title: The Spotlight Survivor
rankings.reasoning: James turned one season into an S+ legacy by winning S12 with a 7-vote jury finish despite taking 9 votes against along the way. He was not a pure challenge steamroller, but his idol play, alliance flexibility, and ability to survive sustained targeting made the win feel earned rather than accidental. As a one-time player, the sample is smaller than the legends around him, but his single-season peak is undeniable.

rankings.name: Mickey
rankings.title: The Nervous Mastermind
rankings.reasoning: Mickey's legacy is still enormous: a champion, a runner-up, and one of the rare players with multiple seasons of real endgame equity. His S10 was the first true blemish, as he joined Bowie's Movement but overplayed his fear of Zoey's likability and gave Bowie the excuse to cut him in Episode 3. Even that early exit reads less like incompetence than two masterminds recognizing each other too quickly; Mickey saw the game, but Bowie moved first.

rankings.name: Cameron
rankings.title: The Brainiac
rankings.reasoning: Reliable challenge contributor (6 total wins). Strong advantage game (2 idols/advantages found). High-end strategist (best rank: #3). Cast tiebreaker for Emma. (S3). Voted Zoey at FTC. (S4). Across 2 season(s): avg placement 4.00.

rankings.name: Wayne
rankings.title: The Golden Goofball
rankings.reasoning: Wayne’s career arc is the cleanest glow-up in the top tier: a loyal S9 bruiser who got advantage-whacked at F9, then came back in S13 and won the whole thing. His first run was defined by bad timing — losing an Extra Vote to Knowledge is Power, missing Shot in the Dark, and watching idols and extras rewrite his boot — but his winning season turned that chaos into resilience. With 3 effective idol plays, 7 alliances, and fan-favorite equity, Wayne proved the lovable shield could also be the endgame equalizer.

rankings.name: Julia
rankings.title: The Ice-Cold Influencer
rankings.reasoning: Julia remains one of the franchise’s sharpest non-winners, combining ruthless social reads with real challenge power across two deep runs. In S9, she manipulated Raj into leaving with an idol, helped form the Misfits to topple Ripper, and still had enough pull to assist the Priya blindside after Bowie stripped her idol with Knowledge is Power. Her S13 runner-up finish cemented her as an elite endgame threat, though her flashiness and threat level kept her from turning dominance into a crown.

rankings.title: The Chaos Engine
rankings.reasoning: Scott remains one of the franchise's most dangerous non-winners: four seasons, four merge-level runs, 8 challenge wins, and a résumé built on lies, leverage, and survival. In S10, he turned a first-impression rejection into fuel, convinced Hicks to hand over an idol, won Say Uncle, and blew up The Three Torches by betraying Jasmine. The problem is that Scott's games leave fingerprints and enemies everywhere; once he exposed Bowie as the power center, Bowie and The Oath had every reason to make him the next warning shot.
rankings.name: Scott
Millie
rankings.title: The Overachiever
rankings.reasoning: S9's most complete non-winner. Millie converted a Beware package into the Red Idol, used it at F8 to nullify a five-vote strike on Priya, and held her alliances together through one of the season's messiest post-merges. She won F5 immunity and was the last woman standing going into the finale. Her game had everything a winning résumé needs — she just ran into Bowie.

rankings.title: The Truth-Serum Titan
rankings.reasoning: Jasmine turned two forgettable mid-game exits into one of the franchise's most satisfying winner arcs. S10 showcased everything her earlier games only hinted at: elite reads, stubborn loyalty, late challenge power, and the ability to stand beside strategists without becoming their pawn. She won Safety Without Power, claimed an Extra Vote, took key immunities in African Lying Safari, Get a Clue, and The Princess Pride, then beat Bowie's architecture outright in the Rejected Olympic Relay. Her game was not the cleanest strategically, but it had the strongest finish and the clearest soul.
rankings.name: Jasmine

rankings.title: The Glow-Up Queen
rankings.reasoning: Lindsay won S1 with pure social grace and timing, beating Alejandro 5-4 while taking only 1 vote all season. She returned to reach the S2 Finale (lost 1-5-1 to Duncan) and then suffered a brutal S7 first boot (6-2-2). With a 33.3% win rate and 6 lifetime jury votes, her résumé is built on likability and trust webs. Rivalries with Jasmine/Shawn in S7 and minimal advantage play left her exposed in fast starts.
strengths: Durable alliance webs that scale to the endgame
Threat calibration without needing challenge runs
weaknesses: Vulnerable to early consensus boots in new casts
Light advantage usage and low challenge reliance
Can be labeled expendable when tribes seek calm
rankings.name: Lindsay
Ryan
rankings.title: The Gentle Wrecking Ball
rankings.reasoning: Ryan's franchise profile is power with a conscience: three strong placements, 5 challenge wins, and enough social gravity to keep finding endgame lanes. In S10, he built The Oath, bonded deeply with Zoey, Carrie, and Brick, and stacked late immunities in Bridal Brawls, Rapa Phooey, and Super Hero-ld. But his heart pulled him in too many directions, especially as his loyalty to Carrie damaged trust elsewhere. By final four, he was too strong to drag and too exposed to save.

rankings.name: Duncan
rankings.title: The Delinquent
rankings.reasoning: CHAMPION season on the résumé. WINNER of S2. Legit jury respect (5 finalist votes). 5-3 vote. (S1). Won Final Tribal 5-1-1. (S2). Across 3 season(s): avg placement 7.33.

rankings.name: Riya
rankings.title: The Polarizing Survivor
rankings.reasoning: Riya’s legacy is built on surviving heat that would have cooked almost anyone else. Her S12 runner-up game absorbed 14 votes against, produced two immunity wins and an idol play, and still carried her to the finals — but the same confrontational style limited her to 2 jury votes against James. Returning for S13 and finishing 4th reinforced the pattern: dangerous, durable, always central, but rarely easy for a jury to reward.

rankings.title: The Badge-Wielding Bulldozer
rankings.reasoning: MacArthur’s S7 win was a controlled power-social run, blending idol security, cross-bloc relationships, and blunt-force command of the endgame. She found the Mauhu idol, managed the Sky rivalry without letting it consume her, and closed with Cody and Carrie while taking only 5 votes that season. Later returns were messier, with 7th and 10th place finishes adding more target equity than trophy equity, but her peak remains one of the franchise’s most respected winning games.
rankings.name: MacArthur
Jake
rankings.title: The Heartline Survivor
rankings.reasoning: Jake’s legacy is built on emotional gravity: the Thom showmance made him one of S11’s central characters, but also kept a spotlight on every vulnerability. He improved from 7th to 3rd in S13, using social bonds, four alliances, and an effective idol play to become a real endgame presence despite never winning a challenge. The résumé is messy — 12 votes against and zero jury votes — but his arc from exposed romantic player to finalist-adjacent survivor gives him undeniable franchise weight.

rankings.name: Emma
rankings.title: The Comeback Kid
rankings.reasoning: CHAMPION season on the résumé. WINNER of S3. Reliable challenge contributor (5 total wins). High-end strategist (best rank: #2). 4-3-1 tiebreaker victory. (S3). Lost via rocks in a deadlock. (S6). Across 3 season(s): avg placement 9.33.

rankings.title: The Soft-Power Magnet
rankings.reasoning: Zoey is one of the franchise's purest jury threats, with a runner-up finish, a fourth place, 8 challenge wins, and 6 jury votes proving her ceiling. Her S10 game was quieter but familiar: people trusted her instinctively, from Gwen to Ryan to Cody to Sanders, which made her both protected and terrifying. Mickey targeted her early for exactly that reason, and the merge open vote confirmed the danger of being everyone's favorite person. She left gracefully after losing to Sanders, but her championship case remains one betrayal or immunity away.
rankings.name: Zoey
Sanders
rankings.title: The Redemption Final Boss
rankings.reasoning: Sanders has now delivered two high-end non-winning games with completely different flavors. S8 was her complete-player showcase, with two idol plays, a MacArthur save against a five-vote majority, and a 4-4 jury tie loss to Alejandro. S10 turned her into a Redemption Island legend after a messy merge vote: five straight duel wins in precision, memory, balance, slingshot, and bocce before returning as the player nobody wanted at the end. Her honor is real, but so is the target it creates once momentum gathers.

rankings.name: Miriam
rankings.title: The Grandmother Gambit
rankings.reasoning: Miriam’s S11 win remains one of the franchise’s sharpest single-season achievements, built on timing, alliance coverage, and just enough advantage power to survive a dangerous endgame. She was never a ghost — 9 total votes against shows opponents saw her coming — but she converted pressure into the season’s defining victory. Her S13 flameout in 19th damages the career average, yet the win, idol, and strategist reputation still keep her comfortably in S-tier territory.

rankings.title: The Underestimated Hub
rankings.reasoning: Carrie's best games are built on making herself look softer than her position actually is. In S7, she was the social hub who balanced Cody and MacArthur, used a Vote Steal at final five, and reached FTC with a credible connector résumé. In S10, she again stayed in rooms where decisions happened, but her bond with Ryan became both her shield and her spotlight. Bowie recognized too late that her kindness could matter to a jury, then cut her before that story could reach the finale.
rankings.name: Carrie

rankings.title: The Reluctant Radar
rankings.reasoning: Cody's gift is awareness: he hears secrets, spots fractures, and usually knows where the game is heading before the vote proves him right. His S7 runner-up game was a connector masterclass with well-timed Legacy and idol plays, while S10 showed the same instincts through his Bowie intel, Mickey vote, merge idol find, and refusal to abandon Priya. The issue is conversion; too often his reads become warnings instead of commands. By final six, he had the information, but not the coalition, and Sanders ended his Redemption shot.
weaknesses: Reads do not always become control
rankings.name: Cody
Ally
rankings.title: The Idol-Cached Almost
rankings.reasoning: Ally’s two-season résumé is loaded with tools: a 3rd-place S12 finish, an 8th-place S13 follow-up, two challenge wins, and three idols overall. Her Hunter connection gave her story a personal anchor, but her game was more than a showmance — she repeatedly had protection, options, and legitimate late-game equity. The issue is finishing power: for all the idols and positioning, she never turned a strong setup into a finalist seat or jury support.

rankings.name: Jay
rankings.title: The Loyal Heart
rankings.reasoning: Navigated the merge rock draw. (S6). Finalist - 0 jury votes. (S6). Across 1 season(s): avg placement 3.00.

rankings.title: The Athlete
rankings.reasoning: Sky's S5 was a challenge-forward run to a tied FTC loss, and in S7 she co-piloted with MacArthur before a late split and a 6th-place cut. She owns 4 total challenge wins and 2 idols across her career, with only 6 votes against. Her Mauhu backbone role helped shape merge plans like targeting Pete and Kitty. The late fracture with MacArthur and muted jury sway kept her off the podium in S7.
strengths: Stable alliance play with power players (MacArthur, Tom)
Composed midgame targeting and vote splits
weaknesses: Can be overshadowed by louder résumés
Limited control of final jury narratives
rankings.name: Sky
Brick
rankings.title: The Noble Shield
rankings.reasoning: Brick's S10 run transformed him from a respectable merge boot into a legitimate endgame character. He played with decency in a season full of knives, protecting Josee, forming key bonds with Tyler and Jasmine, and staying central enough to influence votes without looking like the obvious commander. His late partnership with Jasmine helped remove Ryan and reshape the finale path, but it also made his jury appeal impossible to ignore. At final three, the jury did not eliminate a goat; they eliminated the person who might win on character alone.

rankings.name: Aiden
rankings.title: The Social Safety Net
rankings.reasoning: Aiden’s game is defined by survivability: 5th in S12 and 7th in S13, with enough social coverage to keep finding lanes through dangerous votes. He paired likability with practical protection, adding two challenge wins, three idols, and an effective advantage play across his career. Still, his legacy sits just below the true closers — 14 votes against, no finalist seat, and no jury votes make him feel more like an excellent connector than a winning architect.

rankings.name: Thom
rankings.title: The Clean Runner-Up
rankings.reasoning: Thom’s peak game was one of the cleanest losses on the board: a 2nd-place finish with only a single vote against him that season. He combined two challenge wins, an idol, multiple alliances, and the Jake showmance into a run that felt socially insulated and emotionally central. His S13 return was much shorter at 13th, but even then he only picked up one vote, reinforcing how rarely people actually wanted him gone.

rankings.name: Alec
rankings.title: The Quiet Endgamer
rankings.reasoning: Alec’s legacy is built on steady positioning rather than fireworks, with a 4th-place finish in S11 and a solid 6th-place return in S13. He was rarely the loudest force in the game, but he consistently survived deep into competitive seasons without idol protection. The downside is that 12 votes against and no jury support suggest he was respected more as a survivor than as a closer.

rankings.name: Fiore
rankings.title: The Tiny Arsonist
rankings.reasoning: Fiore’s S11 run was chaos with purpose: she reached 3rd while absorbing heavy heat, burning through two idols, and staying alive when cleaner social players might have collapsed. Her game was never subtle, but it was resourceful, fearless, and impossible to ignore. S13 exposed the limits of that threat level with a 14th-place exit, leaving her as a dangerous almost-finalist whose reputation became hard to outrun.

rankings.title: The Spy
rankings.reasoning: Tom played low-visibility until it mattered: he found the merge idol and fired it at F7 to cancel five votes and bounce Taylor, then was boxed out at F5 by a vote-steal plus double-idol wall. With 0 challenge wins, 1 idol found, and 13 votes against, his résumé hinged on timing, not volume. His bridges with Sky and MacArthur bought space even when he was out of step. The jury-facing story just wasn't big enough by F5.
strengths: Patience and precision with idol timing
Social links that reduce early and midgame exposure
weaknesses: Reactive gameplay limits jury résumé breadth
Becomes an easy consensus when shields are gone
Few marquee moves beyond the F7 idol shot
rankings.name: Tom
Geoff
rankings.title: The Party Surfer
rankings.reasoning: 7-2 vote. (S1). Won EoE return Ep 13. (S4). Across 3 season(s): avg placement 10.33.

rankings.name: Blaineley
rankings.title: The Media Maven
rankings.reasoning: Rock-draw survival at merge. (S6). Final 5 consensus boot. (S6). Across 2 season(s): avg placement 7.00.

rankings.name: Bridgette
rankings.title: The Surfboard Shield
rankings.reasoning: Bridgette’s résumé is uneven, but her best season showed real endgame value: a 3rd-place finish backed by strong challenge utility and an advantage play. Across three appearances, she stacked up 5 challenge wins, proving she could be more than just a social presence when the game demanded performance. The problem is durability — placements of 14th and 16th around that peak, plus 30 votes against, make her career feel like one great run surrounded by rough wipeouts.

rankings.name: Gabby
rankings.title: The Idol Escape Artist
rankings.reasoning: Gabby’s 5th-place run was messy, loud, and hard to kill. She absorbed 9 votes and had no challenge wins, but two idol plays kept her game alive far longer than her threat level suggested it should have lasted. Her legacy is less about control and more about survival instinct under constant fire.

rankings.title: The Spoiled Princess
rankings.reasoning: Taylor ran S7 social lanes with Jacques and Carrie, flipped to form Lawa #3, and survived repeated shots from Scott/Shawn. She won two immunities and still got idol'd out at F7 despite the majority. With 3 challenge wins and 11 votes against, her threat level was obvious. Rivalries with Tom and the enforcer duo kept her in the crosshairs all merge.
strengths: Shield management while keeping personal threat poised
weaknesses: High visibility as a social hub draws idols and counters
Reliance on tight duos invites targeted strikes
rankings.name: Taylor
Mike
rankings.title: The Many Faces
rankings.reasoning: Eliminated 2-0 (Sierra's perfect idol). (S4). Blindsided while Zoey held immunity. (S3). Across 2 season(s): avg placement 9.00.

rankings.name: Rosa-Maria
rankings.title: The Double Idol Matriarch
rankings.reasoning: Rosa-Maria's S12 game was defined by resilience: 8 votes against her, 2 idols played, and still a 6th-place finish. She was constantly in the line of fire but kept finding ways to buy another round, using advantages and relationships — including her showmance with Oliver — to stay relevant. Without challenge wins or jury traction, though, her run reads more like survival artistry than a winning blueprint.

rankings.name: Chase
rankings.title: The Quiet Closer
rankings.reasoning: Chase's S9 run remains one of the better underdog finalist arcs: the vlogger no one took seriously became a late-game operator who made the right call again and again. He idoled himself at the revote that toppled Hicks, won the Vote Stopper to silence Julia at F4, and grabbed F5 immunity to lock in his finale spot. His S12 return was a hard crash at 16th, but the original run still gives him real legacy weight.

rankings.name: Beth
rankings.title: The Sweetheart
rankings.reasoning: Constant heat all game (26 votes against). Opening rocks send to RI. (S6). Sent to RI on opening rocks; eliminated Episode 3 duel. (S6). Across 3 season(s): avg placement 9.33.
weaknesses: Little evidence of consistent strategic control.
Takes too many votes / limited jury support.

rankings.name: Jen
rankings.title: The Civilian Queenpin
rankings.reasoning: Jen's S8 game was a textbook social power run, leading the Civilians and building one of the season's widest coalitions. She won merge immunity, helped engineer the MacArthur revote blindside at F6, and looked positioned for the finale before the Double Shield idol sequence sent her out at F5. Her S12 return to 9th was quieter, but across two seasons she remains one of the stronger non-winning social architects.
weaknesses: Cut at F5 by Double Shield

rankings.name: Ellie
rankings.title: The Heat Magnet
rankings.reasoning: Ellie has now finished 8th twice, and both runs tell the same story: she is tough enough to survive danger, but never insulated enough to control it. Her S11 game was especially chaotic, taking 15 votes against while still finding an idol play to keep herself alive. With 21 career votes against and two idols, her legacy is resilience under pressure more than clean positioning.

rankings.name: Dwayne
rankings.title: The Dad
rankings.reasoning: Early stability bonds with Geoff/Mickey. (S6). First juror. (S3). Across 2 season(s): avg placement 10.50.

rankings.name: Caleb
rankings.title: The Late Blooming Shield
rankings.reasoning: Caleb’s ranking is carried by the rebound: after being S9’s first true elimination, he came back in S13 and pushed all the way to 5th. His first run ended before he could establish control, especially after missing the Ep4 SID play that might have saved him, but the return showed real social traction with three alliances and an immunity win. He still has no finalist equity or jury votes, but he turned a near-nonfactor debut into a credible endgame résumé.

rankings.name: Ezekiel
rankings.title: The Idol Gambler
rankings.reasoning: Ezekiel’s career is a strange mix of legitimate endgame danger and sudden collapse. His first two runs were strong — 6th and 4th, including a fire-making loss to Alejandro and a memorable idol play on Alejandro at the merge. But the S12 19th-place crash badly dents the résumé, turning him from a sneaky advantage threat into one of the franchise’s more volatile returnees.
strengths: Average 5.0 placement across first two seasons

rankings.name: Gwen
rankings.title: The Shadow Marathoner
rankings.reasoning: Gwen's legacy is a strange mix of elite endurance and constant exposure: 9 challenge wins, 30 votes against, and a franchise-record four consecutive immunities in S4. Her best games came when she could stay quiet, observe the room, and let her physical game carry her deep, but her threat level kept catching up before the finale. S10 was a harsh epilogue, with a lost Journey vote making her Bowie's clean early target before Josee ended her Redemption run by inches.
strengths: Strong Redemption duel win over Sam

rankings.title: The Zombie Guy
rankings.reasoning: Shawn plays intense: S5's Beware Idol/Shot in the Dark gamble backfired, and S7 centered on a feared duo with Scott and a loud feud with Kitty. He exited 9th both times, including a 5-4 knife-edge in S7. With 2 challenge wins, 1 idol found, and 17 votes against, he's a force in motion more than a long-term manager. His rivalry heat made him a message boot once the board stabilized.
strengths: Drives targets and momentum alongside a loyal partner
Physical asset in team and individual phases
Unshaken under pressure in contentious votes
weaknesses: Paranoid gambles that cost votes and leverage
Struggles to re-stabilize after shields leave
rankings.name: Shawn
Trent
rankings.title: The Bruised Strategist
rankings.reasoning: Trent has always been more useful than flashy: a reliable challenge contributor with 4 total wins and enough strategic respect to peak as a high-end planner. His first two outings were sturdy, finishing 7th and 6th with an average placement of 6.5, but his S13 return cratered at 18th and dragged down the legacy. Add in 15 votes against and the Heather animosity from S1, and Trent reads as a solid B-tier player who could never fully stabilize his social position.

rankings.name: Amy
rankings.title: The Mean Twin
rankings.reasoning: Across 1 season(s): avg placement 7.00.

rankings.name: Damien
rankings.title: The Calm Endgame Engineer
rankings.reasoning: Damien’s S9 game was one of the cleanest non-winning arcs in this range: he used Ripper as a merge shield, won the F9 horror immunity, found the Campers Hidden Idol at F7, and reached the fire-making endgame. His F5 double-idol standoff showed nerve, and losing to Bowie at fire is a respectable way to fall just short. The S13 15th-place finish hurts, but 5 challenge wins and an idol across two seasons keep him framed as a dangerous, composed player when he gets runway.

rankings.name: Eureka
rankings.title: The Reward Sparkplug
rankings.reasoning: Eureka landed in the middle of the S11 endgame, finishing 6th with a respectable but not dominant profile. Her one reward win and two alliances suggest she had enough social and challenge utility to matter, even if she never fully seized strategic command. With only 4 votes against, she avoided becoming a constant target until her runway simply ran out.

rankings.name: Sugar
rankings.title: The Pageant Menace
rankings.reasoning: Across 1 season(s): avg placement 5.00.

rankings.name: Lake
rankings.title: The Alliance Hopper
rankings.reasoning: Lake’s lone run to 7th was quietly busy: no challenge wins, but an idol play and five alliances show someone constantly trying to reposition. That activity kept her relevant deep into S12, though it never translated into a firm power base or jury respect. She lands as a solid one-season player with tools, but not yet a defining move.

rankings.name: Scarlett
rankings.title: The Volatile Mastermind
rankings.reasoning: Scarlett’s legacy is split between genuine upside and a disastrous return. A 4th-place finish proves she can navigate deep into a season, and her 2 challenge wins show she was not just a background schemer. But S13’s 20th-place finish is a brutal counterweight, turning her into one of the clearest boom-or-bust players in the B tier.

rankings.name: Jo
rankings.title: The Alpha Athlete
rankings.reasoning: 5-3 revote at F9. (S4). Voted Emma at FTC. (S3). Across 2 season(s): avg placement 8.50.

rankings.name: Hunter
rankings.title: The Covered Connector
rankings.reasoning: Hunter’s two-season résumé is built on access more than authority: he had alliances, a showmance with Julia, and even an effectively played idol, but never converted those pieces into real command. Back-to-back middling finishes at 10th and 9th show a player consistently in the game, yet rarely driving it. With 15 votes against and zero challenge wins, his legacy is of someone socially plugged in but strategically underpowered.

rankings.name: Priya
rankings.title: The Relentless Reboot
rankings.reasoning: Priya has become one of the franchise's most persistent mid-to-endgame threats, finishing 7th and 8th while constantly forcing majorities to account for her. In S9 she was the strategic heartbeat of the New Red majority, and in S10 she added a rugged Redemption arc with three straight duel wins plus an immediate merge immunity. Her problem is visibility: Bowie built two different exits around removing her, and her 23 votes against show how often the game identified her as too dangerous to leave alive.

rankings.name: Kelly
rankings.title: The Fireline Fallen
rankings.reasoning: Kelly’s S6 game had legitimate endgame shape: she found and correctly played the Tadhana idol at the swap, then made it all the way to Final 4 before losing fire-making. That single deep run still carries most of her ranking, because her S13 return was a brutal 22nd-place crash that flattened the momentum. She remains a strong what-if — close enough to the finale to matter, but without the repeat performance to prove it was sustainable.

rankings.name: Gerry
rankings.title: The Volatile Puppeteer
rankings.reasoning: Gerry’s S8 run was chaotic, forceful, and genuinely memorable: he survived pre-merge ties, bonded tightly with Jen, found the merged idol at Final 9, and psychologically wore Heather down until she quit with the Super Idol unused. He played his own idol at Final 5 and came up one round short of the finale, giving him one of the messiest but most active fourth-place games on the board. His S13 21st-place exit hurts the career arc, but the peak remains too explosive to ignore.

rankings.name: MK
rankings.title: The Sly Schemer
rankings.reasoning: S9's stealthiest player. MK stayed low, kept her name off ballots, and won the F8 horror immunity when she needed it most. She never had a bad vote until Bowie's bloc went against her at F7 in a 4-2. A composed, analytical game that went further than it looked from the outside.

rankings.name: Dave
rankings.title: The Neurotic Strategist
rankings.reasoning: Across 1 season(s): avg placement 6.00.

rankings.name: Heather
rankings.title: The Queen Bee
rankings.reasoning: Constant heat all game (27 votes against). Strong advantage game (2 idols/advantages found). 7-2 vote. (S2). 3-2 at Episode 5. (S4). Across 3 season(s): avg placement 10.67.
weaknesses: Takes too many votes / limited jury support.

rankings.name: Dawn
rankings.title: The Aura Casualty
rankings.reasoning: Dawn’s game has always been more about presence than power: she made the S3 jury phase, voted for Scott at FTC, and had enough social positioning to be part of multiple alliances. But her exits tell the harsher story — a consensus target in S3 and only 17th on return in S13. With no idols, no challenge wins, and 13 votes against, she reads as a perceptive player who could see the game better than she could control it.

rankings.name: Owen
rankings.title: The Big Buddy
rankings.reasoning: Third boot after idol play. (S2). 5-1* vote. (S2). Across 2 season(s): avg placement 9.50.
weaknesses: Hasn't converted strong runs into season wins.

rankings.name: Courtney
rankings.title: The Courtroom Wildfire
rankings.reasoning: Courtney plays like every tribal is a closing argument, but across four seasons that intensity has produced more heat than control. She has 41 votes against, no challenge wins, and placements that keep stalling in the early-to-mid game despite flashes of usefulness like her S10 outbreak MVP work. Her latest run captured the full Courtney experience: fake idol chaos, credit-stealing, a landslide boot, and one last exit speech that detonated Cody and Bowie's idol on the way out.

rankings.title: The Sunshine
rankings.reasoning: Kitty thrives in chaos: 3 idols found across seasons and the S7 double-idol masterstroke that erased eight votes and boomeranged Pete out. In S6 she burned protection trying to shape power and got split-voted out; in S7 her feud with Shawn made every round volatile before she was cut at 11th. With 2 challenge wins and 28 votes against, she weaponizes fear and timing more than safety. She can be a loyal spark, but the blowback is immediate once the smoke clears.
strengths: Top-tier idol hunting and high-pressure reads
Creates chaos to reset vote maps in her favor
Protects allies at the exact moment stakes spike
weaknesses: Idol fireworks invite instant follow-up boots
Social blowback after big plays limits longevity
rankings.name: Kitty

rankings.title: The Nature Girl
rankings.reasoning: Laurie lived in conflict and chaos in S7, feuding with Cody and drifting as a minority vote before serving as a merge number. She was finally cut 5-3-2 at 10th when the tribe wanted the heat to cool. With 3 challenge wins and 8 votes against, her presence was loud but rarely central. Ties to Taylor helped, but the Cody war defined her arc.
strengths: Fearless confrontation that can force clarity
Utility as a swing number in messy rounds
Loyalty to specific allies even from the bottom
weaknesses: Volatile social game alienates potential allies
Becomes a distraction voters tire of carrying
Thin jury equity after noisy midgame
rankings.name: Laurie
Ripper
rankings.title: The Flatulent Firestarter
rankings.reasoning: Ripper's legacy is less about clean strategy and more about detonating the board around him. In S9, he idoled out four votes in Episode 2, helped trigger a rock draw, survived a chaotic Shot in the Dark clash with Nichelle, then rode Bowie's protection until Emmah double-voted him out at the merge. His S13 return added another idol and more alliance utility, but a 12th-place finish kept him firmly in the messy middle tier.

rankings.name: Max
rankings.title: The Evil Genius
rankings.reasoning: Across 1 season(s): avg placement 10.00.
strengths: Strong average placement across seasons (deep runs).
weaknesses: Limited challenge contribution compared to the field.

rankings.name: Leshawna
rankings.title: The Real One
rankings.reasoning: 6-1 vote. (S2). Second boot. (S2). Across 2 season(s): avg placement 12.00.
weaknesses: Takes too many votes / limited jury support.
Inconsistent results / early exits drag down average placement.

rankings.name: Emmah
rankings.title: The Fearless Thief
rankings.reasoning: Emmah’s S9 run remains one of the boldest non-winning games on the board. She stole Wayne’s Extra Vote with Knowledge is Power, double-voted Ripper out, helped blindside Raj while he held an idol, and won immunity at Final 7 before finally falling in the Final 5 double-idol crossfire. The S12 first boot hurts badly, but her peak still has real strategic bite.

rankings.name: Axel
rankings.title: The Lumberjack
rankings.reasoning: S9's physical force. Axel survived the Ep2 rock draw, helped execute a clean 3-0 on Nichelle, and won merge immunity in Ep8. She was tight with Bowie and Ripper but found herself on the wrong side of the numbers post-merge and fell 4-1 at F9. Left with a stronger résumé than her placement shows.

rankings.name: Dan
rankings.title: The Middle-Rung Broker
rankings.reasoning: Dan’s S11 showing was a modest but functional midgame run, ending in 9th with two alliances and only 5 votes against. He was connected enough to avoid being an immediate outsider, but he lacked the challenge wins or advantages needed to change his trajectory. His legacy is that of a decent social piece who never became the board’s main player.

rankings.title: The Old-School Coach
rankings.reasoning: Pete played the S7 middle with an Exile extra vote, clashed with Carrie/Kitty, and took a swing at Kitty on Idol Night-only to be the fallback when her double-idol detonated. He left at 12th despite 1 challenge win and 5 votes against. His reads were bold; the board just broke perfectly against him. Links with Taylor and Tom couldn't overcome the post-firewall pivot.
strengths: Grabs and uses advantages to press edges
Aggressive targeting of perceived power players
weaknesses: Feuds painted him as an acceptable casualty
Insufficient shields once plans went public
rankings.name: Pete
Topher
rankings.title: The Host Wannabe
rankings.reasoning: Across 1 season(s): avg placement 11.00.
weaknesses: Limited challenge contribution compared to the field.

rankings.name: Stephanie
rankings.title: The Unspent Fuse
rankings.reasoning: Stephanie's game is defined by weapons she almost gets to use and relationships she almost stabilizes. In S10 she found a Vote Steal, helped form the Chaos Crew, and built enough one-on-one bonds to look dangerous, but her fights and shifting loyalties left her without a firm home. Being eliminated by ambassador agreement instead of a vote feels fitting: the room saw the possible explosion and cut the wire before she could light it.

rankings.name: Lill
rankings.title: The Alliance Collector
rankings.reasoning: Lill’s 10th-place finish undersells one interesting detail: she was tied into three alliances, suggesting real early social reach. The problem is that those bonds did not translate into protection or power once the game tightened. With no wins, no idols, and an early-mid exit, her résumé remains more potential than payoff.

rankings.name: Noah
rankings.title: The Snark King
rankings.reasoning: TRIPLE IDOL TRIBAL (played idol). (S4). 4-3 vote. (S1). Across 2 season(s): avg placement 12.00.
weaknesses: Takes too many votes / limited jury support.
Limited challenge contribution compared to the field.
Inconsistent results / early exits drag down average placement.

rankings.title: The Hunky Helper
rankings.reasoning: Brody's game leaned on grit: in S6 he outlasted heat, returned from Redemption, and even snagged an immunity before getting cut 4-2 at 7th. In S7 he never found structure and became the easy consensus boot at 18th. With 1 challenge win, 33 votes against, and zero idols, his threat came from endurance more than position. Loyal to Geoff and the Surfers vibe, he struggled to shake rival heat from Ryan and Mickey and later MacArthur.
strengths: Loyal duo play with Geoff to secure short-term safety
Can absorb being a decoy target without panicking
weaknesses: Limited numbers and weak majority pathways
Target magnet post-merge once redemption narrative formed
No advantage game to counter coordinated strikes

rankings.reasoning: Held idol without playing it. (S1). 4-3-2 vote. (S1). Across 2 season(s): avg placement 12.50.
weaknesses: Inconsistent results / early exits drag down average placement.

rankings.name: Josee
rankings.title: The Fury Duelist
rankings.reasoning: Josee has never been a clean strategic operator, but she is rarely boring and never soft. Across three seasons, her placements remain stuck in the teens, yet S10 gave her a real fighting chapter: surviving a tiebreak scare, bonding hard with Jasmine and Brick, then beating Gwen and Courtney on Redemption Island. The issue is that her fire makes her visible before it makes her powerful, and Priya finally exposed the ceiling of Josee's rage-fueled survival game.

rankings.name: Karol
rankings.title: The Idol Underdog
rankings.reasoning: Karol at least left S12 with a tool in hand, playing an idol in a season where many lower-tier players never found that kind of agency. The problem is that the advantage did not change her trajectory much: she still landed 11th, took 8 votes, and had only one alliance to lean on. Her game had flashes of resourcefulness, but not enough social insulation to survive the early-middle squeeze.

rankings.name: Eva
rankings.title: The Tempered Equalizer
rankings.reasoning: Eva's first run was a near-nonfactor, ending in a 21st-place boot on a 6-4 vote before she could turn intensity into leverage. Her S13 return gave her a better finish at 11th and a small narrative boost as an equalizer, but she still left without a challenge win, idol, or jury impact. Across two seasons, she feels like a player with obvious physical presence who never quite translated it into game power.
strengths: Never received a vote in S13

rankings.name: Hicks
rankings.title: The Trusting Scanner
rankings.reasoning: Hicks reads the board better than most players ranked around him, spotting idols, tells, cracks, and vote structures before the room fully catches up. His S9 early game had real promise, and S10 doubled down on his information-first style with another idol find and a central role in Contender politics. But his career keeps ending as collateral damage, and handing an idol to Scott before being voted out is the clearest symbol of his gap between awareness and self-protection.

rankings.name: Seraphine
rankings.title: The Alliance Collector
rankings.reasoning: Seraphine is the highest-ranked of this Season 11 early-game cluster, largely because she at least had visible social scaffolding. Three alliances gave her more narrative and strategic texture than most of the boots around her, but an 11th-place finish with 5 votes against shows those connections never translated into real control. She leaves as a C-tier almost-player: present in the game, but not powerful enough to survive the squeeze.

rankings.name: Katie
rankings.title: The BFF

weaknesses: Inconsistent results / early exits drag down average placement.

rankings.name: Yul

rankings.reasoning: Yul’s S12 game has the same surface-level assets as a more dangerous player: an idol, an alliance, and enough presence to draw attention. But the execution never caught up to the threat level, and his idol play could not keep him from sliding out in 12th. With 8 votes against and no challenge wins, he looked more like a target with a trinket than a strategist with a plan.

strengths: High win conversion rate when they play.

rankings.name: Sterling
rankings.title: The One-Link Optimist
rankings.reasoning: Sterling's Season 11 game had the outline of a strategy but not the force to make it matter. A single alliance helped keep him from being totally isolated, yet 12th place and 4 votes against point to a player who was included more than protected. His legacy is modest: socially aware enough to survive the very bottom, but never close to driving the season.

rankings.name: Brightly
rankings.title: The Mad Scientist
rankings.reasoning: S9's most underrated player. Brightly orchestrated the Zee boot on Day 1 and moved immediately to target Emmah as a liability — Hicks flipped and saved Emmah instead, ending Brightly's game at F13. She had the clearest read on the season's social dynamics of any pre-merger but left too early for the résumé to reflect it.

rankings.name: Sam
rankings.title: The Eager Underdog
rankings.reasoning: Sam's ranking still gets a little lift from his odd S3 stat line, where he left by rock draw without ever receiving a vote. S10 was much rougher: he tried to provide camp value and prove he belonged, but one vulnerable tribal turned into a tie, a tiebreak loss, and a first boot trajectory through Redemption. His effort against Gwen in Hand on Idol was sincere, but Sam's game has usually been more heart than leverage.

rankings.name: Izzy
rankings.title: The Chaos Gremlin
rankings.reasoning: 5-4 vote. (S1). Tribe swap casualty. (S1). Across 1 season(s): avg placement 18.00.
weaknesses: Inconsistent results / early exits drag down average placement.

rankings.name: Mary
rankings.title: The Brain
rankings.reasoning: ZERO votes against across appearances. Idoled out at swap vote. (S6). Idol casualty Episode 7 (Kelly's idol negated majority). (S6). Across 1 season(s): avg placement 14.00.
strengths: Low target profile and/or strong jury appeal.
weaknesses: Inconsistent results / early exits drag down average placement.
Limited challenge contribution compared to the field.

rankings.name: Nick
rankings.title: The Quiet Passenger
rankings.reasoning: Nick's Season 11 run reads like someone who was around the action without becoming the action. He had one alliance and only 4 votes against, but a 13th-place exit makes it hard to credit him with meaningful leverage. Not a disaster, just a low-impact game that ended before he could prove there was more underneath.

rankings.name: Raj
rankings.title: The Gentle Giant
rankings.reasoning: S9's most painful exit. Raj secured the Blue Beware advantage in Ep2 and completed his idol — then walked out of Ep7 tribal with it untouched after Julia manipulated the vote around him. He never saw it coming. An unfortunate end to a player with the social tools to go much deeper.

rankings.name: Tess
rankings.title: The Overexposed Outsider
rankings.reasoning: Tess’s S12 run was defined less by moves and more by how quickly the vote count piled up against her. Finishing 13th with 11 votes against is a brutal statistical marker, especially without an idol, challenge win, or alliance note to soften the landing. She was not invisible, but visibility without protection made her one of the season’s easier names to write down.
strengths: Made it past the opening phase

rankings.name: Oliver
rankings.title: The Brief Romantic
rankings.reasoning: Oliver's S12 run had a little more texture than the average early boot thanks to his Rosa-Maria showmance and a pair of alliance connections. But the résumé never really materialized: 14th place, no challenge wins, no idols, and only three votes against before the game moved on without him. He ranks as someone who had social threads, just not enough control or staying power to turn them into traction.

rankings.title: The Loyal Boyfriend
rankings.reasoning: Devin's S7 started with real social traction among the Fans (Carrie, Taylor), but swap attitudes from Favorites hardened and he fell 4-3. With 0 challenge wins, no idols, and 4 votes against, he lacked the shield or advantage to survive Scott/Shawn's push and Jacques' flip. His instincts to connect were sound; the cover wasn't. Once the board framed him as expendable, the window shut fast.
strengths: Quick to build early trust and soft alliances
Comfort playing the middle to keep options open
Solid social reads on Fan dynamics
weaknesses: No protection when cross-tribe heat rises
Couldn't lock a stable majority post-swap
Viewed as expendable by Favorites' core
rankings.name: Devin
Kai
rankings.title: The Overconnected Flameout
rankings.reasoning: Kai managed to get into three alliances in S12, which suggests he was at least trying to build options rather than drift. The problem is that none of those bonds protected him when it mattered, and six votes against is a loud signal that he became an easy target early. A 15th-place finish with no wins or idols leaves him as a player with activity, but very little effectiveness.

rankings.name: Tyler
rankings.title: The Heartfirst Liability
rankings.reasoning: Tyler's S10 run gave him more emotional texture than his disastrous S1 showing, with real bonds to Brick and Jasmine and a clearer sense of loyalty. Unfortunately, the same sincerity that made him likable also made his position easy to map, especially once the Tyler-Jasmine duo became public. Add in Dodgebrawl weakness, a Basic Straining quit, and 11 votes against, and his franchise story remains that of a good-hearted player the game keeps outpacing.

rankings.name: Grett
rankings.title: The Early Lightning Rod
rankings.reasoning: Grett's 14th-place finish is dragged down by one glaring number: 8 votes against in a single season. That kind of heat this early usually means either poor threat management or a social position that collapsed fast. She had enough presence to be noticed, but in Total Drama, being noticed for the wrong reasons is often fatal.
strengths: Survived past the first few boots

rankings.name: Samey

rankings.title: The Hothead
rankings.reasoning: Lorenzo survived the S7 swap by flipping to help boot Brody, but the disciplined Mauhu trio (MacArthur/Sky/Tom) snuffed him 3-2 next. With 0 challenge wins, no idols, and 4 votes against, his opportunism bought only a brief extension. Bonding with Pete was real, but not enough to break structure. Once the majority solidified, he had no wedge.
strengths: Willingness to flip early for survival
Decent read on immediate majority safety
Useful pair dynamics (with Pete) to negotiate
weaknesses: Struggles to convert flips into long-term security

Scary Girl
rankings.title: The Goth
rankings.reasoning: S9's wildcard gone too soon. Scary Girl pulled the short rock at the Ep2 0-0 idol council — the season's most chaotic early moment — and was gone before she had a chance to show what her game was. Impossible to evaluate fairly on placement alone.

rankings.name: Rock
rankings.title: The Rocker
rankings.reasoning: S8's unlucky early exit. Rock got caught in Civilian feud crossfire and was voted out 3-2 in Ep2 — a victim of tribal politics more than anything he did wrong. Left before his game had a chance to open up.

rankings.name: Drew

rankings.name: Connor

With no alliances listed, no challenge wins, and no idols, his ranking reflects a contestant who never got to launch.

rankings.name: Ashley

With 4 votes against, no challenge wins, and no recorded alliance structure, her game was over before it could develop an identity. It is a harsh legacy, but first boots rarely get the chance to be anything else.
